message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier B) for the hc-assay hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-10). Decide TRUE_POSITIVE (genuine production-readiness defect to fix) or FALSE_POSITIVE (false/already-mitigated/out-of-scope-by-design/not real). Out-of-scope: dataset-agnostic opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts (but a NEW leaked PII/secret class IS in scope); on-box data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope. Read real code and REPRODUCE where testable (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ...; "python -m pytest"; gh). Judge ONLY P10-PII-2.

FINDING P10-PII-2 (low): Hostname bare-token rule lacks a leading boundary → substring over-redaction
Location: scripts/capture_transcripts.py:~238 (_hostname_patterns machine-host pattern)
Claim: The machine-hostname pattern is re.escape(b)+r"(?:\.local)?\b" with re.IGNORECASE and NO leading \b, so the bare hostname matches as a SUBSTRING: a host named "host" redacts inside "localhost"→"local[REDACTED]" and "host.docker.internal"; "[REDACTED]" redacts inside "[REDACTED]-backups". Only a len<4 guard exists, which doesn't stop common names (host/build/runner/ubuntu). On this node the hostname is distinctive ([REDACTED]) so live impact is low, and the only test uses a distinctive name so the over-redaction path is untested. VERIFY the substring behavior empirically and that it would corrupt legit content on a common-hostname node. (fix-regression of P9-PII-1)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

273	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
274	    # Redact the machine/infrastructure hostname (#P9-PII-1) — the production node's hostname and
275	    # internal *.local hosts that no other rule covers.
276	    for pat in _HOSTNAME_RES:
277	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
278	    return text

content.text: The pattern at line 238 is `re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b"` with `re.IGNORECASE` and no leading `\b`. Let me reproduce the substring over-redaction empirically.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
import re
# Simulate the machine-host pattern for various common hostnames
for b in ['host','build','runner','ubuntu','[REDACTED]']:
    pat = re.compile(re.escape(b) + r'(?:\.local)?\b', re.IGNORECASE)
    tests = {
        'host': ['localhost','host.docker.internal','myhost'],
        'build': ['rebuild','prebuild','building things'],
        'runner': ['github-runner','frontrunner'],
        'ubuntu': ['ubuntu-latest','xubuntu'],
        '[REDACTED]': ['[REDACTED]-backups','[REDACTED]'],
    }[b]
    for t in tests:
        print(f'{b!r:14} in {t!r:24} -> {pat.sub(\"[REDACTED]\", t)!r}')
    print()
"

content.content:
'host'         in 'localhost'              -> 'local[REDACTED]'
'host'         in 'host.docker.internal'   -> '[REDACTED].docker.internal'
'host'         in 'myhost'                 -> 'my[REDACTED]'

'build'        in 'rebuild'                -> 're[REDACTED]'
'build'        in 'prebuild'               -> 'pre[REDACTED]'
'build'        in 'building things'        -> 'building things'

'runner'       in 'github-runner'          -> 'github-[REDACTED]'
'runner'       in 'frontrunner'            -> 'front[REDACTED]'

'ubuntu'       in 'ubuntu-latest'          -> '[REDACTED]-latest'
'ubuntu'       in 'xubuntu'                -> 'x[REDACTED]'

'[REDACTED]'   in '[REDACTED]-backups'     -> '[REDACTED]-backups'
'[REDACTED]'   in '[REDACTED]'       -> '[REDACTED]'

content.text:
Substring over-redaction confirmed. The trailing `\b` only anchors the end; there is no leading boundary, so the bare hostname matches mid-word. Note `'building things'` was NOT redacted because `\b` requires the end of "build" to be a word boundary, and "build" inside "building" is followed by "i" (no boundary) — but `rebuild`/`prebuild` (end-of-string boundary) and `localhost` (host at end) are corrupted.

Let me verify the live hostname on this node and check the test coverage to confirm the over-redaction path is untested.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && { scutil --get LocalHostName 2>/dev/null; scutil --get ComputerName 2>/dev/null; python -c "import platform; print(platform.node())"; }; echo "---tests---"; grep -rn "hostname\|_hostname_patterns\|ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS\|\.local\|localhost\|P9-PII" /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests 2>/dev/null | head -40

content.text: None of those are the hostname-scrubber tests. Let me find the actual test for the hostname patterns in capture_transcripts.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rln "capture_transcripts\|_hostname_patterns\|hostname" tests/ | head; echo "---"; grep -rn "host\|hostname\|macmini\|\.local" tests/test_capture_transcripts.py 2>/dev/null | head -30

content.content:
199:def test_scrub_redacts_machine_and_namespace_hostnames(monkeypatch):
200:    # #P9-PII-1: the production node's hostname and internal *.local hosts are operator/infra PII
201:    # that no email/username/handle rule covered. The machine hostname is redacted bare + .local;
202:    # an ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS namespace host has its .local form redacted while the bare project token
203:    # (which appears in legit paths/repo refs) is preserved, and unrelated "*.local" is untouched.
204:    monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS", "hc-proj")
205:    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_host")
206:    # force a deterministic machine hostname regardless of the test host
207:    monkeypatch.setattr(cap.socket, "gethostname", lambda: "buildbox-7")
209:    cap._HOSTNAME_RES = cap._hostname_patterns()
211:        "ran on buildbox-7.local (bare buildbox-7); ns https://hc-proj.local/prov#; "
212:        "repo hc-proj/app and settings.local.json"
214:    assert "buildbox-7" not in out  # machine hostname redacted (bare + .local)
215:    assert "hc-proj.local" not in out  # namespace host .local form redacted
217:    assert "settings.local.json" in out  # unrelated *.local untouched (no blanket rule)
220:def test_precommit_hook_wires_namespace_host_into_capture():
221:    # #P9-PII-1 confirm-concern: the namespace host ([REDACTED]) only redacts when ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS
222:    # names it; the machine hostname auto-derives but this does not. The pre-commit hook must set
223:    # ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS before running capture, else every commit re-introduces [REDACTED].
225:    assert "ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS" in hook, "pre-commit hook does not wire the namespace host scrub"
228:    assert "ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS" in pre, "ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS set after capture (too late)"

content.text:
Confirmed. The only test uses `"buildbox-7"` — a distinctive name where substring over-redaction wouldn't surface as corruption of common words. The test asserts `"settings.local.json" in out` but never tests a hostname that is a substring of legitimate content. The over-redaction path is untested.

Now let me verify the actual impact severity: on THIS node the hostname is `[REDACTED]` (distinctive), so live impact is low as the finding states. But the bug is a real fix-regression: the analogous username/display-name/handle rules — does the prior P9 rule design intend bare matching? Let me confirm the regression framing by checking whether a leading boundary is the correct fix and whether it would still match the live hostname.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python3 -c "
import re
# Proposed fix: add leading boundary
for b in ['host','[REDACTED]']:
    pat = re.compile(r'(?<![\w.-])' + re.escape(b) + r'(?:\.local)?\b', re.IGNORECASE)
    for t in ['localhost','host.docker.internal','myhost','ran on [REDACTED]','[REDACTED]-backups','bare [REDACTED] here']:
        print(f'{b!r:12} {t!r:30} -> {pat.sub(\"[REDACTED]\", t)!r}')
    print()
"

content.content:
'host'       'localhost'                    -> 'localhost'
'host'       'host.docker.internal'         -> '[REDACTED].docker.internal'
'host'       'myhost'                       -> 'myhost'
'host'       'ran on [REDACTED]'      -> 'ran on [REDACTED]'
'host'       '[REDACTED]-backups'           -> '[REDACTED]-backups'
'host'       'bare [REDACTED] here'         -> 'bare [REDACTED] here'

'[REDACTED]' 'localhost'                    -> 'localhost'
'[REDACTED]' 'host.docker.internal'         -> 'host.docker.internal'
'[REDACTED]' 'myhost'                       -> 'myhost'
'[REDACTED]' 'ran on [REDACTED]'      -> 'ran on [REDACTED]'
'[REDACTED]' '[REDACTED]-backups'           -> '[REDACTED]-backups'
'[REDACTED]' 'bare [REDACTED] here'         -> 'bare [REDACTED] here'

content.text:
The proposed leading-boundary fix preserves the legitimate `[REDACTED]` redaction (the P9 intent) while eliminating substring over-redaction of `localhost`, `myhost`, `host.docker.internal`'s non-host parts, etc.

I have everything I need to render a verdict.

input.reasoning:
Verified empirically against the real code at scripts/capture_transcripts.py:238. The machine-host pattern is `re.compile(re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b", re.IGNORECASE)` with NO leading word boundary, so the bare hostname matches as a substring. Reproduced: hostname 'host' redacts inside 'localhost'→'local[REDACTED]', 'myhost'→'my[REDACTED]'; 'build'→'rebuild'/'prebuild' corrupted; 'runner'→'github-runner'/'frontrunner' corrupted; 'ubuntu'→'ubuntu-latest'/'xubuntu' corrupted; '[REDACTED]'→'[REDACTED]-backups'→'[REDACTED]-backups'. The only guard is `len(b) < 4`, which does not stop common hostnames (host/build/runner/ubuntu all >=4). This is a genuine correctness defect: it corrupts legitimate transcript content (over-redaction destroys data), and is precisely a fix-regression of P9-PII-1 (the rule was added to redact the infra hostname but lacks a boundary anchor).

Scope/severity check: the finding self-rates as low and explicitly notes live impact on THIS node is low because the actual hostname is '[REDACTED]' (distinctive, confirmed via scutil --get LocalHostName/ComputerName → '[REDACTED]'). That is honest scoping, not a disqualifier — the defect is real for any node with a common hostname, and the substring corruption is undesirable behavior in committed code.

Test-coverage claim verified: the sole test (tests/test_scripts.py:199 test_scrub_redacts_machine_and_namespace_hostnames) forces a distinctive hostname 'buildbox-7' and asserts 'settings.local.json' survives, but never exercises a hostname that is a substring of legitimate content, so the over-redaction path is untested — matching the finding.

Confirmed a correct fix exists and is non-destructive: adding a leading boundary (e.g. `(?<![\w.-])`) preserves the intended P9 redaction of '[REDACTED]' and bare '[REDACTED]' while leaving 'localhost'/'myhost'/'[REDACTED]-backups' untouched. This is a real, in-scope, fixable production-readiness defect (the new/affected class is over-redaction corrupting content, a legitimate transcript-scrubber correctness issue).

content.text: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The hostname bare-token rule at scripts/capture_transcripts.py:238 lacks a leading word boundary, causing reproducible substring over-redaction (e.g., `localhost`→`local[REDACTED]`, `[REDACTED]-backups`→`[REDACTED]-backups`). It is a genuine fix-regression of P9-PII-1, the over-redaction path is untested, and a non-destructive leading-boundary fix is available.
